subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Section 14.1 is entirely about constructing geometric shapes with an unmarked ruler and compass — perpendicular bisectors, right angles, angle bisectors, copies of an angle, parallel lines, 60°/30°/15°/120° angles, and regular hexagons — which is precisely the drawing and constructing of lines, perpendiculars, parallels, angles, and simple figures with specified properties using compass and straightedge.

The chapter applies properties of angles and polygons to make its constructions work — that angles around a point sum to 360°, that an equilateral triangle's angles are 60° so six of them form a regular hexagon with 120° angles, and the angle relationships in the 6-pointed star — outlining and applying polygon and angle properties to solve construction problems.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 14.2 is a full treatment of tiling — covering regions and the whole plane with 2 × 1 tiles, squares, equilateral triangles, and regular hexagons — and the chapter constructs decorative designs (petalled figures, trefoil and pointed arches) and shows tiling in Indian and world art, Escher's work, and bee hives, which is the construction of tiling designs and appreciation of their appearances in art.

Every construction is justified deductively through triangle congruence (the perpendicular bisector via SSS then SAS, angle bisection and angle-copying via SSS), and the tiling section proves impossibility results — a 5 × 7 grid cannot be tiled because 35 is odd, and the black-and-white colouring argument shows regions with unequal colour counts are untileable — sustained convincing argument.

The chapter invites creative problem-solving through the Chinese tangram puzzles, the optical-illusion figure, the many open-ended design constructions, and especially the inventive black-and-white colouring trick that makes a hard tiling question easy — an appreciation of finding one's own clever solutions.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to construct perpendicular bisectors, right angles, angle bisectors, copied angles, parallel lines, and regular hexagons with only a ruler and compass, and to apply polygon and angle properties, justifying each construction through the congruence of triangles.
